body.question_1: Reflecting on the full academic year, how would you rate your overall educational experience at this university?
body.answer_1: My overall experience in the Computer Science program at the School of Information Technology during this academic year has been decent, with meaningful highs and some notable lows that I believe the institution has both the capacity and responsibility to address. The academic content, when delivered well, is genuinely rigorous and relevant, and I have formed valuable relationships with peers and some excellent faculty members. However, structural issues — inconsistent teaching quality, administrative inefficiencies, and gaps in student support — have at times undermined what could otherwise be an exceptional educational experience. With targeted improvements and a genuine commitment to acting on student feedback, this institution has the foundations to become truly outstanding.

body.question_2: How effectively do you believe this year's coursework has prepared you for your future career or further academic studies?
body.answer_2: Career preparation support from the Career Center at the School of Information Technology has been disappointing and has left many students — including myself — feeling ill-equipped for the competitive graduate employment market. Career workshops are infrequent, poorly promoted, and often scheduled at times that conflict with lecture commitments, reducing student participation. The internship database is outdated and contains many positions that are no longer active, and support for students during internship placements is minimal to nonexistent. I recommend the university to substantially reinvest in the Career Center — expanding its employer network, modernizing its tools, and increasing the frequency and quality of career development programming across all academic programs and year levels.

body.question_3: How would you assess the progression of your academic skills and knowledge throughout this academic year?
body.answer_3: The Computer Science curriculum at the School of Information Technology appears to lag behind current industry standards in several significant areas that I believe require urgent review and revision. Many courses continue to teach outdated tools and methodologies that are rarely used in professional settings today, leaving students underprepared for actual workplace demands. The curriculum also lacks sufficient elective depth and specialization pathways, limiting students' ability to develop expertise in niche areas of growing importance. I suggest the Academic Affairs Office and school leadership to conduct a thorough curriculum audit in close consultation with industry professionals and alumni to ensure course content remains relevant, rigorous, and career-oriented.

body.question_4: How satisfied are you with the extracurricular activities, student clubs, and personal development opportunities available?
body.answer_4: The extracurricular opportunities available at the School of Information Technology, coordinated by the Student Affairs Office, are remarkable and represent a genuinely important complement to the formal academic curriculum. A wide and diverse range of student clubs, cultural societies, sports teams, and volunteer programs are actively supported and well-resourced by the university, enabling students to develop leadership skills, build social connections, and pursue interests outside their academic programs. Events are well-promoted, regularly scheduled, and inclusive in their design, welcoming students from all backgrounds and programs to participate equally. I commend the institution's recognition that holistic student development extends beyond classroom performance and into every dimension of campus life.

body.question_5: How well has the university's Career Center prepared you for internship opportunities and graduate employment?
body.answer_5: Career preparation services at the School of Information Technology, offered through the Career Center, are acceptable and meet some student needs while falling short in others. Basic resume guidance and periodic career fairs provide a foundation, but more intensive and personalized support would greatly benefit students planning to enter a competitive and rapidly evolving job market. The internship program has room for significant growth — expanding the employer partner network, improving placement matching, and providing structured mentorship during internship periods would make it substantially more valuable. Regular industry panels and alumni engagement events would also meaningfully bridge the gap between academic preparation and professional workplace expectations.

body.question_6: How would you rate the university's financial services, including tuition fee management and scholarship support?
body.answer_6: The financial services provided by the Finance Department at the School of Information Technology have been excellent this semester, and I genuinely applaud the clarity, transparency, and efficiency with which they are managed. Fee statements are issued promptly and accurately, with clear breakdowns of tuition, facility charges, and any applicable scholarship deductions that make it easy to verify and plan payments. Payment options are flexible and well-communicated, including installment plans that meaningfully reduce financial pressure on students and families. Scholarship disbursements are processed on time and confirmed through official notifications, removing uncertainty around critical financial support. The finance office staff are professional, patient, and genuinely helpful when navigating complex financial queries.

body.question_7: How would you evaluate the IT infrastructure and digital services provided by the university over the course of the year?
body.answer_7: The IT infrastructure and digital learning environment provided by the IT Support Department is excellent and has greatly supported my studies at the School of Information Technology this semester. Campus Wi-Fi coverage is consistently strong and reliable across all academic buildings, laboratories, and study spaces, enabling seamless online collaboration and research activities throughout the day. Computer labs are well-equipped with up-to-date hardware and licensed software relevant to my field, reducing the burden on students to source tools independently. The IT helpdesk is responsive and knowledgeable, resolving technical issues promptly and minimizing disruption to student workflows. I acknowledge the university's ongoing investment in digital infrastructure.

body.question_8: How satisfied are you with the library resources — both physical and digital — that were available throughout the academic year?
body.answer_8: Learning resources for my Computer Science program at the School of Information Technology are reasonable, with some courses providing excellent, well-curated materials and others offering minimal guidance beyond the core required textbook. The library's digital collection is reasonable in scope but has some notable gaps in specialized resources relevant to advanced topics in our field. The LMS functions adequately as a distribution platform, though its use varies significantly between lecturers, creating an inconsistent experience across modules. A more standardized approach to resource provision, with minimum content requirements per course, would considerably improve the consistency and quality of the learning environment.

body.question_9: How effectively did the university communicate important information, deadlines, and updates throughout the academic year?
body.answer_9: The academic administrative processes managed by the Academic Affairs Office at the School of Information Technology have been impressive this semester, and I praise the efficiency and transparency with which they are handled. Course registration was smooth and intuitive via the student portal, with clear instructions provided well in advance of enrollment deadlines. Grade appeals and transcript requests are processed promptly and communicated through official channels with regular status updates that reduce student anxiety. Administrative staff are approachable, knowledgeable, and genuinely helpful when students encounter difficulties navigating university policies or procedures. The overall administrative experience reflects a student-centered approach that I believe enhances the broader university experience significantly.

body.question_10: What are your key recommendations for improving the overall student experience in the coming academic year?
body.answer_10: My key recommendation for the School of Information Technology is to urgently address the substandard systemic issues that have affected Computer Science students this semester, rather than treating them as isolated incidents. A structured, transparent student feedback mechanism — where concerns are acknowledged within a set timeframe and outcomes communicated back to students — would significantly rebuild trust that has been eroded by unaddressed issues. I also strongly urge leadership to conduct a comprehensive curriculum and service quality review in genuine consultation with current students, alumni, and industry partners. Without meaningful action on the concerns raised in this feedback, student satisfaction and the institution's competitive standing will continue to decline in the years ahead.
